questions.q: A healthcare office's policy and procedures manual should be reviewed and updated:
options: Every five years
Only when new staff are hired
Annually or when regulations change
Only during accreditation reviews
questions.explain: Most medical offices review written policies at least yearly and whenever laws, payer rules, or clinical regulations change.
optionExplanations: A 5-year cycle is too infrequent; procedures and regulations change more often.
Hiring does not determine when policies must be updated; compliance changes can occur anytime.
Annual review plus updates when regulations change keeps procedures current and compliant.
Accreditation reviews are not the only trigger; policies should be current before any survey.

questions.q: Which scheduling method is most effective for minimizing patient wait time while maximizing provider efficiency?
options: Wave scheduling
Double booking
Open hours scheduling
First-come, first-served
questions.explain: Wave scheduling books several patients at the start of an hour and then staggers others, reducing idle provider time while limiting long waits when managed correctly.
optionExplanations: Wave scheduling balances flow by grouping arrivals and using the hour to work through them efficiently.
Double booking often increases wait times and can overwhelm staff unless used only for specific short visits.
Open hours encourages walk-ins but can create unpredictable surges and longer waits.
First-come, first-served ignores visit length and provider availability, increasing bottlenecks.

questions.q: A tickler file in healthcare administration is primarily used to:
options: Store patient billing information
Provide reminders for future actions or follow-ups
Track medical errors
Record staff attendance
questions.explain: A tickler file is a reminder system for time-sensitive tasks such as follow-up calls, authorizations, renewals, or reports due.
optionExplanations: Billing records belong in the accounting or practice management system, not a tickler file.
Tickler files are designed to prompt staff to complete future actions on a specific date.
Medical error tracking is part of quality/risk management systems, not a tickler file.
Attendance tracking is an HR function and uses timekeeping systems.

questions.q: Which is the most important step when implementing a new electronic appointment system?
options: Distributing login credentials without training
Conducting staff training and testing the system before launch
Eliminating paper backups immediately
Posting instructions for patients online only
questions.explain: Training staff and testing workflows before go-live prevents scheduling errors, lost appointments, and downtime.
optionExplanations: Credentials alone do not ensure correct use; untrained users increase errors and support requests.
Training and pre-launch testing validate templates, permissions, and day-to-day workflows.
Removing paper backups immediately can be risky during transition; contingency plans are needed.
Patient-facing instructions do not replace internal staff readiness and system testing.

questions.q: To maintain HIPAA compliance, a sign-in sheet in a medical office should:
options: Include only the patient's name and appointment time
List diagnosis information for billing
Display insurance numbers for efficiency
Be posted on a public bulletin board
questions.explain: HIPAA requires minimizing unnecessary disclosure; a sign-in sheet should not expose diagnoses or identifiers beyond what is needed to manage flow.
optionExplanations: Name and appointment time are typically sufficient for check-in without revealing protected details.
Diagnoses are protected health information and should not be displayed on a sign-in sheet.
Insurance numbers are identifiers and should not be visible to other patients.
Public display increases unauthorized disclosure risk and is not appropriate.

questions.q: When a physician is unexpectedly absent, the best first step for the office manager is to:
options: Cancel all appointments immediately
Notify patients, offer rescheduling, and reassign urgent cases to other providers
Postpone all patient visits until the physician returns
Ask patients to wait in the lobby indefinitely
questions.explain: The office should communicate quickly, preserve access for urgent needs, and re-route care rather than leaving patients without options.
optionExplanations: Immediate cancellation without triage can delay urgent care and harm patient satisfaction.
Notify and reschedule, and ensure urgent cases are covered by another qualified provider or facility.
Blanket postponement ignores clinical urgency and continuity of care needs.
Keeping patients waiting without a plan is unsafe and unprofessional.

questions.q: The prefix brady- in medical terminology means:
options: Fast
Slow
Irregular
Absent
questions.explain: In medical terminology, the prefix 'brady-' means slow (e.g., bradycardia = slow heart rate).
optionExplanations: 'Tachy-' is the prefix for fast, not brady-.
Brady- means slow.
Irregular is commonly described by 'arrhythmia' or similar terms, not brady-.
Absent would be described with terms like 'a-' or 'an-' depending on the word.

questions.q: The medical term hepatomegaly refers to:
options: Inflammation of the kidney
Enlargement of the liver
Disease of the pancreas
Hardening of the arteries
questions.explain: Hepato- refers to the liver and -megaly means enlargement, so hepatomegaly is enlargement of the liver.
optionExplanations: Kidney inflammation is nephritis (nephr- = kidney, -itis = inflammation).
Hepatomegaly literally means enlarged liver.
Pancreas disease uses pancreat- as the root (e.g., pancreatitis).
Hardening of arteries is arteriosclerosis/atherosclerosis.

questions.q: The root word derm refers to:
options: Skin
Heart
Bone
Lung
questions.explain: The root 'derm' means skin, as in dermatology.
optionExplanations: Derm refers to skin tissue.
Heart is cardi-.
Bone is oste-.
Lung is pneum- or pulmon-.

questions.q: The suffix -ectomy means:
options: Surgical removal
Inflammation
Enlargement
Disease
questions.explain: The suffix '-ectomy' means surgical removal of a body part (e.g., appendectomy).
optionExplanations: -ectomy indicates surgical removal.
Inflammation is -itis.
Enlargement is -megaly.
Disease is -osis in many terms.

questions.q: A patient diagnosed with tachycardia has:
options: Slow breathing rate
Rapid heart rate
High blood pressure
Low blood sugar
questions.explain: Tachycardia means an abnormally rapid heart rate (tachy- = fast, -cardia = heart condition).
optionExplanations: Breathing rate relates to terms like tachypnea or bradypnea, not tachycardia.
Tachycardia specifically refers to a fast heart rate.
High blood pressure is hypertension.
Low blood sugar is hypoglycemia.

questions.q: Which law protects patient privacy and governs the use of health information?
options: OSHA
HIPAA
ADA
HITECH
questions.explain: HIPAA sets national standards for protecting individually identifiable health information and regulating its use and disclosure.
optionExplanations: OSHA focuses on workplace safety, not patient privacy.
HIPAA is the primary U.S. law governing privacy and security of health information.
ADA addresses disability discrimination and accommodations.
HITECH strengthened health IT and HIPAA enforcement, but HIPAA is the core privacy law.

questions.q: Informed consent requires that the patient:
options: Signs a form without explanation
Understands the risks, benefits, and alternatives to treatment
Gives consent verbally only
Agrees after the procedure has started
questions.explain: Informed consent means the patient receives understandable information and voluntarily agrees after understanding risks, benefits, and alternatives.
optionExplanations: A signature without explanation is not informed consent.
Understanding risks, benefits, and alternatives is the key requirement for informed consent.
Consent may be written and sometimes verbal, but it must still be informed and documented appropriately.
Consent must be obtained before the procedure, not after it begins.

questions.q: The Good Samaritan Law generally protects:
options: Medical professionals from malpractice claims in their workplace
Individuals who provide emergency care in good faith without compensation
Hospitals from liability in patient disputes
Patients from paying for emergency services
questions.explain: Good Samaritan laws generally protect people who render emergency aid in good faith, typically outside a formal care setting, from certain liability.
optionExplanations: Workplace malpractice coverage depends on professional standards and insurance, not Good Samaritan laws.
These laws are intended to encourage emergency assistance by limiting liability when acting in good faith.
Hospitals have separate liability rules; Good Samaritan laws are not a blanket shield for facilities.
Payment for emergency services is governed by other laws and policies, not Good Samaritan laws.

questions.q: Which is an example of a breach of confidentiality?
options: Sharing patient details with another provider involved in care
Discussing a patient's diagnosis in a public elevator
Reviewing a patient's chart during treatment
Obtaining patient consent for information release
questions.explain: Discussing identifiable patient information in a public place is an unauthorized disclosure and violates confidentiality.
optionExplanations: Sharing information for treatment with involved providers is generally permitted under HIPAA.
Public discussion where others can overhear is a common confidentiality breach.
Chart review for treatment is a permitted use when access is role-appropriate.
Releasing information with proper patient authorization is permitted.

questions.q: Under HIPAA, the minimum necessary standard generally applies when a covered entity uses or discloses PHI for which purpose?
options: Treatment
Payment
Healthcare operations
All uses and disclosures, including treatment
questions.explain: HIPAA's minimum necessary standard applies to most uses/disclosures for payment and operations, but it generally does not apply to disclosures for treatment.
optionExplanations: Treatment disclosures are typically exempt from minimum necessary to avoid interfering with care.
Payment-related use/disclosure is subject to minimum necessary, so this is not the best choice alone.
Healthcare operations are subject to the minimum necessary requirement in most cases.
Minimum necessary does not apply to all disclosures (e.g., treatment exemptions).

questions.q: Which document is most commonly used to authorize release of a patient's protected health information to a third party?
options: Advance directive
Informed consent form
HIPAA authorization
Incident report
questions.explain: A HIPAA authorization is the specific written permission that allows disclosure of PHI to named recipients for stated purposes.
optionExplanations: Advance directives address future care decisions, not routine PHI disclosure.
Informed consent covers permission for treatment/procedures, not general PHI release.
A HIPAA authorization is designed for releasing PHI to third parties outside routine treatment/payment/operations.
Incident reports document events; they are not patient authorizations.

questions.q: In revenue cycle management, which step occurs immediately after services are documented and coded?
options: Claim submission to the payer
Patient registration
Charge capture/entry
Payment posting
questions.explain: After documentation and coding, charges are captured/entered into the billing system before the claim is transmitted.
optionExplanations: Claims are submitted after charges are captured and prepared in the system.
Registration happens before the visit and before coding.
Charge capture/entry is the immediate step that turns coded services into billable charges.
Payment posting happens after the payer processes the claim.

questions.q: What is the primary purpose of a denial management process in a medical office?
options: To reduce clinical documentation requirements
To appeal or correct rejected/denied claims to obtain payment
To eliminate patient copays
To assign ICD-10 codes automatically
questions.explain: Denial management focuses on identifying why claims were denied and taking corrective action (e.g., appeals, corrections) to recover reimbursement.
optionExplanations: Denial management does not reduce documentation needs; it often highlights documentation gaps.
Correcting and appealing denials is the core objective to secure appropriate reimbursement.
Copays are set by benefit plans and patient responsibility rules, not denial workflows.
Coding tools may help, but denial management is broader than automatic code assignment.

questions.q: Which is the best example of protected health information (PHI) under HIPAA?
options: A patient's appointment time listed by last name only
A de-identified dataset with all 18 identifiers removed
A lab result linked to a patient's name or medical record number
A newspaper article about influenza season
questions.explain: PHI includes individually identifiable health information; a lab result tied to a patient identifier qualifies as PHI.
optionExplanations: A sign-in list using only last name can still be identifying; however, this example is ambiguous compared with an explicit identifier plus clinical data.
Properly de-identified data is not PHI.
Clinical data linked to an identifier (name or MRN) is PHI.
General news is not patient-specific health information.

questions.q: Which type of insurance plan typically requires the patient to choose a primary care provider (PCP) and obtain referrals for specialists?
options: PPO
HMO
Indemnity fee-for-service
Workers' compensation
questions.explain: HMOs commonly use a gatekeeper PCP model where referrals are needed for many specialist services.
optionExplanations: PPOs usually allow self-referral to specialists, though incentives may apply.
HMOs typically require a PCP and referrals to manage access and costs.
Indemnity plans generally allow broad choice without referral structures.
Workers' compensation is a coverage type for work-related injuries, not a referral-based network model.

questions.q: In medical billing, what does the term 'copayment' mean?
options: A fixed amount the patient pays at the time of service
A percentage of allowed charges the patient pays
The amount the payer refuses to cover
A provider discount applied before billing
questions.explain: A copayment (copay) is a set dollar amount the patient owes for a covered service, often due at check-in or check-out.
optionExplanations: Copays are fixed-dollar obligations set by the health plan.
A percentage paid by the patient is coinsurance, not a copay.
A payer refusal to cover is a denial or non-covered charge.
Provider discounts are contractual adjustments, not copays.

questions.q: Which identifier is primarily used to link all documentation for a specific patient within an electronic health record (EHR) system?
options: CPT code
Medical record number (MRN)
National Drug Code (NDC)
Tax identification number (TIN)
questions.explain: The MRN is the patient-specific identifier used by the facility/practice to tie encounters, notes, labs, and billing to the correct person.
optionExplanations: CPT codes describe services, not patients.
MRNs are used to uniquely identify and link a patient's record within a practice or facility.
NDC identifies medication products, not patients.
A TIN identifies a business entity for tax and billing, not a patient.

questions.q: What is the best action if a patient requests a copy of their medical records and the office has a HIPAA-compliant process in place?
options: Refuse unless the patient explains why they need the records
Provide access within the required timeframe after verifying identity and documenting the request
Release records only to the patient's employer
Tell the patient to obtain the records from the insurance company
questions.explain: HIPAA grants patients the right to access their records; offices should verify identity, document the request, and provide access within required time limits.
optionExplanations: Patients generally do not have to justify a request for their own records.
Verification, documentation, and timely access are core requirements for compliant release to the patient.
Employers are not automatically entitled to records and usually require a valid authorization.
Insurers may have claims data, but the provider is responsible for the designated record set they maintain.

questions.q: Which federal program primarily provides health coverage to individuals age 65 and older and certain disabled individuals?
options: Medicaid
Medicare
TRICARE
CHIP
questions.explain: Medicare is the federal health insurance program for most people 65+ and some younger people with disabilities or ESRD.
optionExplanations: Medicaid is needs-based and jointly funded by states and the federal government.
Medicare is primarily age- and disability-based federal coverage.
TRICARE serves military members and families.
CHIP covers eligible children in low-income families who do not qualify for Medicaid.

questions.q: What is the main purpose of an Advance Beneficiary Notice (ABN) in Medicare billing?
options: To enroll a patient in Medicare Part D
To notify a patient that Medicare may not pay for a service and the patient may be responsible
To authorize release of PHI
To document patient satisfaction
questions.explain: An ABN informs a Medicare beneficiary that a service may be non-covered, allowing the patient to decide and accept financial responsibility if they proceed.
optionExplanations: Part D enrollment is handled through plan selection, not an ABN.
ABNs are used to warn of possible noncoverage and shift liability appropriately when rules are followed.
PHI release uses HIPAA authorizations, not ABNs.
Patient satisfaction surveys do not determine Medicare coverage liability.

questions.q: Which action best reduces the risk of patient misidentification during check-in?
options: Asking 'Are you John Smith?' and waiting for confirmation
Verifying at least two identifiers such as full name and date of birth
Relying on the appointment schedule alone
Using room number as the identifier
questions.explain: Best practice is to verify two patient identifiers (e.g., full name and DOB) rather than using a leading question or a single source.
optionExplanations: Leading questions can prompt incorrect confirmation, especially in busy waiting rooms.
Two identifiers reduce the chance of mixing up patients with similar names.
Schedules can contain errors and do not confirm identity in the moment.
Room numbers are logistical details, not patient identifiers.

questions.q: Which office report would most directly help a manager identify excessive no-show rates by provider or appointment type?
options: A/R aging report
Appointment status report (scheduled, checked-in, canceled, no-show)
General ledger
Inventory usage log
questions.explain: Appointment status reports track outcomes like no-shows and cancellations and can be filtered by provider, location, or visit type.
optionExplanations: A/R aging is for unpaid balances by time bucket, not scheduling outcomes.
Appointment status data directly measures no-show patterns.
General ledger summarizes accounting transactions, not appointment outcomes.
Inventory logs track supplies, not attendance.

questions.q: In medical coding, which code set is primarily used to report procedures and services performed by physicians and other providers?
options: ICD-10-CM
CPT
HCPCS Level II only
SNOMED CT
questions.explain: CPT codes are the standard for reporting many medical procedures and professional services in the U.S.
optionExplanations: ICD-10-CM is primarily used to report diagnoses/reasons for visits, not most professional procedures.
CPT codes describe many professional procedures and services on outpatient/physician claims.
HCPCS Level II mainly covers supplies, equipment, and certain services (e.g., ambulance); it does not replace CPT for most procedures.
SNOMED CT is a clinical terminology used in records, not the standard procedure code set for claims submission.

questions.q: Which code set is primarily used to report a patient's diagnosis on outpatient and professional claims?
options: ICD-10-CM
CPT
NDC
DRG
questions.explain: ICD-10-CM codes describe diagnoses and reasons for visits on outpatient/professional claims.
optionExplanations: ICD-10-CM is used to code diagnoses in the U.S.
CPT codes procedures/services, not diagnoses.
NDC identifies drug products, not diagnoses.
DRGs are inpatient payment groupers, not outpatient diagnosis codes.

questions.q: What does an accounts receivable (A/R) aging report primarily show?
options: Patient satisfaction scores by month
Outstanding balances grouped by how long they have been unpaid
Daily appointment volume
Employee hours worked
questions.explain: An A/R aging report categorizes unpaid balances into time buckets (e.g., 0-30, 31-60, 61-90 days) to prioritize follow-up and collections.
optionExplanations: Satisfaction scores are gathered via surveys, not A/R aging.
A/R aging focuses on unpaid balances and their age.
Appointment volume is shown in scheduling/encounter reports.
Employee hours are tracked by timekeeping systems.

questions.q: Which is the best example of a 'pre-authorization' requirement?
options: A patient pays a copay at check-in
The payer must approve a planned MRI before it is performed
The patient signs a HIPAA authorization
The provider posts a payment to an account
questions.explain: Pre-authorization means the insurer requires approval in advance for certain services to be covered.
optionExplanations: Copays are patient cost-sharing, not prior approval.
Many plans require prior authorization for high-cost imaging like MRIs.
HIPAA authorizations relate to information release, not coverage approval.
Posting a payment is an accounting action, not an approval requirement.

questions.q: In a medical office, which task is most appropriate to delegate to a trained medical assistant rather than the billing specialist?
options: Posting EOB payments
Verifying insurance eligibility
Taking and recording vital signs
Submitting electronic claims
questions.explain: Taking vital signs is a clinical support duty commonly performed by medical assistants, while billing tasks are handled by billing staff.
optionExplanations: Posting EOB payments is a billing/accounts task.
Eligibility verification is often a front-desk/billing function, not a clinical task.
Vital signs collection is within typical medical assistant scope (with training and policy compliance).
Claim submission is a billing function.

questions.q: Which statement best describes the purpose of OSHA in a healthcare workplace?
options: It governs patient privacy disclosures
It sets workplace safety standards to reduce hazards and injuries
It determines insurance reimbursement rates
It licenses physicians
questions.explain: OSHA focuses on protecting workers by regulating safety practices (e.g., bloodborne pathogen standards, sharps safety).
optionExplanations: Patient privacy is governed by HIPAA and related laws.
OSHA sets and enforces workplace safety standards.
Reimbursement is set by payer contracts and regulations, not OSHA.
Professional licensing is handled by state boards.

questions.q: Which action best supports compliance with the OSHA Bloodborne Pathogens Standard?
options: Storing used sharps in regular trash bins
Using approved sharps containers and documenting exposure control procedures
Allowing food in specimen processing areas
Reusing single-use needles after cleaning
questions.explain: Proper sharps disposal and an exposure control plan are central OSHA requirements to reduce occupational exposure risk.
optionExplanations: Sharps must go into puncture-resistant sharps containers, not regular trash.
Sharps containers and a documented exposure control plan are required elements of compliance.
Eating in contaminated areas increases exposure risk and is prohibited by policy.
Reusing needles is unsafe and violates standard precautions and policy.

questions.q: What is the primary purpose of standard precautions in a healthcare setting?
options: To treat every patient as if they have a highly contagious airborne disease
To assume all blood and certain body fluids are potentially infectious and use protective measures accordingly
To eliminate the need for hand hygiene
To allow reuse of personal protective equipment (PPE) indefinitely
questions.explain: Standard precautions require treating blood and certain body fluids as potentially infectious and using hand hygiene and PPE based on anticipated exposure.
optionExplanations: Airborne precautions are a specific transmission-based category and are not assumed for all patients.
Standard precautions assume potential infection risk for blood/body fluids and guide PPE/hand hygiene use.
Hand hygiene is a core component, not eliminated.
PPE use follows manufacturer and infection control guidance; indefinite reuse is not appropriate.

questions.q: Which field on an insurance card most directly helps a biller route a claim to the correct payer system?
options: Group number and member ID
Patient height and weight
Pharmacy pickup location
Appointment date
questions.explain: Member ID and group number identify the insured and plan, helping the office submit claims to the correct payer and plan product.
optionExplanations: Group and member IDs are key identifiers for eligibility and claim submission.
Height and weight are clinical data, not payer routing information.
Pharmacy location is unrelated to medical claim routing.
The appointment date is needed on a claim but does not identify the payer or plan.

questions.q: Which is the best description of a payer Explanation of Benefits (EOB)?
options: A marketing brochure from the insurer
A statement showing how a claim was processed, including allowed amounts and patient responsibility
A prescription label
A provider's internal audit report
questions.explain: An EOB summarizes claim adjudication, including what was billed, allowed, paid, adjusted, and what the patient may owe.
optionExplanations: EOBs are not marketing materials; they are claim processing statements.
This is the correct definition of an EOB.
Prescription labels relate to dispensing medications.
Internal audits are provider tools and are not payer EOBs.

questions.q: Which scheduling metric most directly reflects access to care for new patients?
options: Average days to third next available appointment
Percentage of claims denied
Inventory turnover ratio
Number of staff meetings held
questions.explain: Third next available appointment is a common access metric because it reduces distortion from last-minute openings or cancellations.
optionExplanations: This metric is widely used to assess appointment access and backlog.
Denial rates relate to billing performance, not scheduling access.
Inventory turnover is a supply metric, not patient access.
Meetings do not measure access to care.

questions.q: When scanning paper documents into an EHR, what practice best supports accurate indexing and retrieval?
options: Scanning all documents into one large file with no labels
Using standardized document types, dates, and patient identifiers during indexing
Saving files on personal desktops for quick access
Deleting originals immediately without quality checks
questions.explain: Standardized indexing (document type, date, patient) improves searchability and reduces misfiled records.
optionExplanations: Unlabeled bulk files are hard to retrieve and increase risk of missing information.
Standard document taxonomy and accurate patient/date indexing support reliable retrieval.
Personal desktops are insecure and bypass retention and access controls.
Quality checks should confirm readability and correct patient assignment before destroying originals.

questions.q: Which statement best describes the difference between a copayment and coinsurance?
options: Copayment is a percentage; coinsurance is a fixed amount
Copayment is a fixed amount; coinsurance is a percentage of allowed charges
They are the same concept
Coinsurance applies only to prescription drugs
questions.explain: A copayment is typically a fixed dollar amount, while coinsurance is a percentage of the allowed amount after deductibles are met (depending on plan).
optionExplanations: This reverses the definitions.
This matches standard definitions used in most health plans.
They are different forms of cost sharing.
Coinsurance can apply to many medical services, not only prescriptions.

questions.q: Which patient balance is most appropriate to collect at check-in when the office has verified eligibility and benefits?
options: The entire estimated charge for the visit regardless of benefits
The required copay and any known past-due balance
No payment of any kind
Only the deductible for future visits
questions.explain: Collecting the known copay and any confirmed outstanding balance at check-in improves cash flow and reduces later collections while staying consistent with plan rules.
optionExplanations: Collecting the full charge can violate payer contracts and is usually unnecessary.
Copay and confirmed past-due balances are standard to collect at check-in when policy allows.
Many practices collect copays; skipping all payments increases receivables.
Deductibles may apply, but collecting 'only' deductible for future visits is not a standard approach.

questions.q: Which practice best supports confidentiality when leaving a voicemail for a patient?
options: Stating the patient's diagnosis and test results in detail
Leaving only minimal information and a callback number without sensitive details
Asking a neighbor to deliver the message
Texting PHI to an unverified number
questions.explain: Best practice is to limit voicemail content to minimal necessary information and request a callback, unless the patient has explicitly agreed to receive detailed messages.
optionExplanations: Detailed clinical information can be overheard and may violate privacy expectations.
Minimal information with a callback number reduces the risk of unauthorized disclosure.
Sharing through a neighbor is not appropriate.
Unverified texting can expose PHI and is not a secure method without safeguards and consent.

questions.q: What is the primary purpose of role-based access controls in an EHR?
options: To speed up internet connections
To ensure users can access only the minimum information needed for their job duties
To eliminate the need for passwords
To allow patients to edit clinical notes freely
questions.explain: Role-based access limits system permissions to what each job role requires, supporting privacy and security.
optionExplanations: Internet speed is unrelated to access controls.
Limiting access by role is a core security principle and supports HIPAA safeguards.
Passwords are still needed; role-based controls govern authorization, not authentication.
Patient portals may allow limited input, but patients do not freely edit provider notes in standard workflows.

questions.q: In a typical clinic workflow, which task should occur before a claim is submitted to reduce denials?
options: Post payments
Verify patient demographics and insurance eligibility
Send the account to collections immediately
Close the practice for the day
questions.explain: Correct demographics and verified eligibility reduce front-end errors that commonly cause claim rejections and denials.
optionExplanations: Payment posting happens after adjudication, not before submission.
Eligibility and demographic verification are key front-end steps to reduce denials.
Collections is a late-stage step, not a pre-submission step.
Closing the practice is unrelated to claim quality.

questions.q: Which is the best description of a 'clean claim'?
options: A claim submitted on paper only
A claim that is complete, accurate, and meets payer requirements for processing without manual intervention
A claim with no patient responsibility
A claim that includes only diagnosis codes
questions.explain: A clean claim has all required information and valid codes so the payer can process it without requesting corrections.
optionExplanations: A claim can be clean whether sent electronically or on paper; paper-only does not define cleanliness.
A clean claim has all required data and valid codes so it can be processed without requests for correction.
Clean claims can still result in copays/deductibles/coinsurance; patient responsibility does not make a claim unclean.
Claims require procedures/services and other fields; diagnosis-only claims are incomplete and often rejected.

questions.q: Which office policy most directly reduces the risk of a HIPAA privacy incident at the front desk?
options: Calling out full diagnoses in the waiting room
Positioning computer screens away from public view and using privacy filters when needed
Sharing passwords among staff for convenience
Printing schedules and leaving them on unattended counters
questions.explain: Shielding screens from public view and using privacy filters reduces incidental disclosure of PHI to other patients and visitors.
optionExplanations: Announcing diagnoses publicly is a clear privacy risk.
Screen positioning and privacy filters are practical safeguards against incidental disclosure.
Shared passwords undermine audit trails and access controls.
Unattended printed schedules can expose PHI.

questions.q: Which is the best definition of 'protected health information' (PHI) in an office setting?
options: Any information about the office building
Individually identifiable health information in any form (paper, electronic, or oral)
Only electronic lab results
Only information created by a physician
questions.explain: PHI includes identifiable health information in any medium, not just electronic records or physician-created notes.
optionExplanations: Building information is not health information about a person.
This matches the broad HIPAA concept of identifiable health information across media.
PHI is not limited to electronic lab results.
PHI can be created by many workforce members, not only physicians.

questions.q: A patient requests that their spouse be allowed to discuss billing questions with the office. What is the most appropriate administrative step?
options: Refuse all discussion with anyone but the patient forever
Document the patient's permission using the office's authorized contact process or a written authorization as required
Give the spouse full portal access without verification
Discuss billing details in the waiting room to save time
questions.explain: Offices typically document permitted contacts or obtain appropriate authorization so staff can verify who is allowed to receive information.
optionExplanations: Patients can authorize others; a blanket refusal is not required.
Documenting permission and verifying identity supports privacy compliance while meeting the patient's request.
Granting portal access requires formal identity verification and permissions; it should not be done informally.
Public discussions risk unauthorized disclosure.

questions.q: Which metric best measures how quickly a practice collects money owed after services are provided?
options: Days in accounts receivable (days in A/R)
Number of exam rooms
Average patient age
Number of procedure codes billed
questions.explain: Days in A/R reflects the average time it takes to collect payment after charges are posted and is a key revenue cycle performance indicator.
optionExplanations: Days in A/R directly measures collection speed.
Exam rooms affect capacity, not collection timing.
Patient age does not measure collections.
Number of codes billed measures volume, not collection speed.

questions.q: Which action best helps prevent duplicate patient records in an EHR?
options: Creating a new record for every visit
Searching for an existing patient and matching identifiers before creating a new chart
Allowing patients to choose any MRN they want
Disabling all demographic fields
questions.explain: Duplicate records are reduced by careful search and verification (name, DOB, address, phone) before creating a new record.
optionExplanations: Creating new records each time guarantees duplicates.
Searching and verifying identifiers prevents duplicates and safety risks.
MRNs are system-assigned, not chosen by patients.
Disabling demographics removes the data needed to match patients correctly.

questions.q: What is the primary purpose of a Notice of Privacy Practices (NPP) in a healthcare office?
options: To list physician salaries
To explain how the practice may use and disclose PHI and the patient's privacy rights
To provide medical advice
To advertise office hours only
questions.explain: HIPAA requires covered entities to provide an NPP that describes uses/disclosures of PHI and patient rights.
optionExplanations: Salaries are not the purpose of the NPP.
The NPP explains PHI handling and patient rights under HIPAA.
Medical advice is provided by clinicians, not in the NPP.
Office hours may be included elsewhere, but the NPP is about privacy practices.

questions.q: Which is the best example of healthcare operations under HIPAA?
options: A nurse consulting another nurse about a patient's medication
A practice conducting a quality improvement review of appointment wait times
A patient requesting their lab results
A provider ordering a lab test
questions.explain: Quality assessment and improvement activities are considered healthcare operations under HIPAA.
optionExplanations: Consultation about medication is treatment, not operations.
Quality improvement is a classic healthcare operations activity.
Patient access requests are patient rights, not operations.
Ordering labs is treatment.

questions.q: Which front-desk action most directly improves claim acceptance on first submission?
options: Skipping insurance verification to save time
Collecting complete demographics and scanning the current insurance card
Entering only the patient's first name
Using last year's policy information
questions.explain: Accurate demographics and current payer information (member ID, group, payer address/portal) reduce rejections due to eligibility and data errors.
optionExplanations: Skipping verification increases errors and rework.
Complete demographics and current insurance data are among the highest-impact clean-claim steps.
Incomplete names increase matching and billing errors.
Old policy information can cause eligibility failures and claim rejections.

questions.q: What is the main reason practices perform insurance eligibility verification before the visit?
options: To diagnose the patient
To confirm coverage, effective dates, benefits, and patient cost-sharing
To change the patient's plan
To eliminate the need for coding
questions.explain: Eligibility verification confirms whether services are covered and what the patient's financial responsibility will be, reducing surprises and denials.
optionExplanations: Diagnosis is a clinical function, not an insurance verification purpose.
Coverage, benefits, and cost-sharing confirmation are the core goals.
Practices cannot change a patient's plan through verification.
Coding is still required for billing.

questions.q: Which schedule template best supports a mix of short follow-up visits and longer new-patient exams?
options: Using the same time length for every visit type
Using visit-type-specific time blocks (e.g., 15-min follow-up, 30-45-min new patient)
Booking only walk-ins
Scheduling all long visits at the end of the day with no breaks
questions.explain: Template design that matches appointment length to visit type improves flow, reduces waiting, and helps providers stay on time.
optionExplanations: Equal lengths for all visits ignores complexity differences and creates delays.
Visit-type-specific blocks align time needed with services provided and improve efficiency.
Only walk-ins is unpredictable and can increase wait times.
Stacking long visits at the end can create late running and staff overtime.

questions.q: Which statement about documentation supports medical necessity for billing purposes?
options: Only the diagnosis code matters
Documentation should support why the service was needed and what was performed
Billing staff can create clinical documentation after the fact
Medical necessity is determined only by patient request
questions.explain: Medical necessity requires documentation of the reason for service and the work performed, consistent with payer rules and coding standards.
optionExplanations: Procedure coding and documentation matter; diagnosis alone is not sufficient.
Accurate documentation connecting reason and service supports medical necessity.
Clinical staff must document; billing staff should not fabricate documentation.
Patient request alone does not establish medical necessity under payer policies.

questions.q: What is a common purpose of a medical office incident report?
options: To bill the patient for an error
To document unusual events (e.g., falls, exposure, adverse events) for risk management follow-up
To replace the patient's medical record
To advertise services
questions.explain: Incident reports capture facts about unexpected events to support investigation, corrective action, and safety improvement.
optionExplanations: Incident reports are not billing tools.
They document events for risk management and quality improvement.
They do not replace clinical documentation in the medical record.
They are not marketing materials.

questions.q: Which best describes a 'chain of custody' requirement in healthcare administration?
options: A process for tracking who handled a specimen or evidence item from collection to final disposition
A method for scheduling surgeries
A rule for setting copays
A way to file insurance claims faster
questions.explain: Chain of custody documents transfers and handling to maintain integrity and traceability, often used for lab specimens in legal contexts or controlled substances.
optionExplanations: This is the correct definition and purpose.
Scheduling is unrelated to custody tracking.
Copays are benefit design features, not custody rules.
Claims processing speed is unrelated to custody documentation.

questions.q: Which is the best example of a 'breach' under HIPAA?
options: A staff member accesses the EHR only for assigned patients
An encrypted laptop is stolen and the encryption keys are not compromised
Unsecured PHI is emailed to the wrong recipient outside the practice
A provider discusses treatment with another provider involved in care
questions.explain: Sending unsecured PHI to the wrong recipient is an unauthorized disclosure and is a common reportable breach risk depending on the assessment.
optionExplanations: Role-appropriate access for assigned patients is permitted.
If strong encryption is in place, the incident may not meet the definition of a breach because PHI is not unsecured.
Unsecured PHI sent to an unauthorized recipient is a likely breach scenario.
Treatment discussions among involved providers are generally permitted uses/disclosures.

questions.q: Which policy best supports secure disposal of documents containing PHI?
options: Placing them in open recycling bins
Shredding or using a locked shredding bin with an approved destruction process
Taking them home to dispose later
Leaving them on the printer for staff to pick up
questions.explain: Secure disposal requires a controlled process (locked bins, shredding, vendor agreements) to prevent unauthorized access to PHI.
optionExplanations: Open bins allow unauthorized access to PHI.
Locked shredding and verified destruction reduce disclosure risk and support compliance.
Taking PHI home increases risk and is usually prohibited by policy.
Unattended printouts can expose PHI to the public or unauthorized staff.

questions.q: What is the main purpose of an internal audit trail in an EHR?
options: To reduce appointment times
To record user actions (viewing, editing) for security monitoring and compliance
To set insurance premiums
To automatically diagnose patients
questions.explain: Audit trails log access and changes, helping detect inappropriate access, support investigations, and meet security requirements.
optionExplanations: Audit trails do not shorten visits directly.
Logging user activity supports monitoring and compliance.
Premiums are set by insurers, not EHR audit trails.
EHRs do not diagnose automatically; clinicians do.

questions.q: Which is the best definition of 'credentialing' in a healthcare practice context?
options: Training a new employee on office policies
Verifying a provider's qualifications and enrolling them with payers
Assigning ICD-10 codes
Ordering medical supplies
questions.explain: Credentialing involves verifying licensure, education, and training, and often includes payer enrollment so services can be billed.
optionExplanations: Policy training is onboarding, not credentialing.
This matches common healthcare administration usage of credentialing and payer enrollment.
Coding is separate from credentialing.
Supply ordering is an inventory function.

questions.q: Which process most directly helps ensure that laboratory test results are attached to the correct patient's chart?
options: Entering results without identifiers to save time
Using barcoded specimen labels and verifying patient identifiers at collection and result posting
Allowing patients to label specimens themselves without verification
Posting all results into a shared folder
questions.explain: Barcode labeling and identifier verification at key handoffs reduce mix-ups and support accurate result posting.
optionExplanations: Missing identifiers increases misfile risk.
Barcode labeling with two-identifier verification is a standard safety practice.
Patient-only labeling without verification is unsafe.
Shared folders are insecure and do not ensure correct chart association.

questions.q: Which is a common consequence of incomplete demographic data (e.g., missing address or DOB) in the billing system?
options: Faster claim processing
Increased claim rejections and difficulty collecting patient balances
Automatic upgrades in insurance coverage
Reduced need for authorization
questions.explain: Missing demographics often lead to eligibility issues, claim rejections, returned statements, and collections problems.
optionExplanations: Incomplete data does not speed processing; it causes rework.
Payers often reject claims with missing or mismatched demographics, and billing statements may not reach patients.
Coverage upgrades do not occur from missing data.
Authorization requirements are plan rules, not reduced by missing demographics.

questions.q: Which communication technique is most appropriate when explaining a billing statement to a patient who is confused about charges?
options: Use as many technical abbreviations as possible
Explain line items in plain language and confirm understanding
Refuse to answer and refer them to the payer for all questions
Discuss another patient's bill as an example
questions.explain: Clear, plain-language explanations reduce confusion and complaints, and checking understanding ensures the patient knows next steps.
optionExplanations: Technical abbreviations can increase confusion.
Plain-language explanations with teach-back/confirmation is best practice.
Some payer questions may require the payer, but offices should explain their own charges and provide guidance.
Discussing another patient's bill would be a privacy violation.

questions.q: Which is the best definition of a 'deductible' in health insurance?
options: A fixed amount paid per visit
The amount the patient must pay out of pocket before the plan begins paying for covered services (subject to plan rules)
The portion the provider writes off
A penalty for missing appointments
questions.explain: A deductible is the amount the insured pays before coverage begins paying, though many plans exempt preventive services or apply different rules.
optionExplanations: Fixed per-visit payments are copays.
This is the standard definition of a deductible.
Provider write-offs are contractual adjustments, not deductibles.
No-show penalties are office fees, not insurance deductibles.

questions.q: Which is the best way to reduce long check-in lines during peak clinic hours?
options: Require every patient to fill forms on paper each visit
Use pre-registration, online forms, and verify insurance before arrival
Schedule all patients for the same time
Turn off the appointment reminder system
questions.explain: Pre-registration and online forms shift work earlier, shorten front-desk time, and reduce congestion.
optionExplanations: Repeating paper forms increases front-desk workload.
Pre-registration and advance verification reduce on-site processing time.
Scheduling everyone at the same time increases congestion.
Reminder systems reduce no-shows and help manage flow; turning them off can worsen inefficiency.

questions.q: Which term describes the process of matching incoming payments to specific charges and updating the patient's account balance?
options: Charge capture
Payment posting
Utilization review
Clinical triage
questions.explain: Payment posting applies payer and patient payments (and adjustments) to charges so balances and A/R are accurate.
optionExplanations: Charge capture records billable services, not payments.
Payment posting records and applies payments/adjustments to accounts.
Utilization review evaluates medical necessity/appropriateness, not posting payments.
Clinical triage prioritizes patient care needs, not accounting.

questions.q: Which is the best description of an 'allowed amount' on an EOB?
options: The provider's full charge
The maximum amount the payer recognizes for the service under the contract
The patient's deductible amount
The office's supply cost
questions.explain: The allowed amount is the contracted or payer-determined amount on which payment and patient responsibility are calculated.
optionExplanations: Charged amount can be higher than allowed; it is not the same.
Allowed amount is the payer-recognized amount used to calculate reimbursement.
Deductible is patient responsibility, not the allowed amount definition.
Supply cost is internal and unrelated to payer allowed amounts.

questions.q: Which is an example of a contractual adjustment in medical billing?
options: A patient paying a copay
Writing off the difference between the provider's charge and the payer's allowed amount per contract
A physician documenting a diagnosis
A patient no-show fee
questions.explain: Contractual adjustments reflect required write-offs under payer agreements when charges exceed the allowed amount.
optionExplanations: Copays are patient payments, not adjustments.
The contractual write-off is a standard adjustment tied to payer contracts.
Clinical documentation is not an accounting adjustment.
No-show fees are office charges, not contractual adjustments.

questions.q: Which scheduling reminder method typically reduces no-show rates most effectively while staying privacy-conscious?
options: No reminders at all
Automated reminders (text/call/email) with minimal PHI and an easy way to confirm or reschedule
Posting patient names and times on social media
Only mailing reminders one day before the visit
questions.explain: Automated reminders sent ahead of time, with minimal details and convenient response options, are commonly associated with fewer no-shows and fewer last-minute cancellations.
optionExplanations: No reminders generally increases no-shows.
Automated, privacy-conscious reminders allow patients to act and reduce missed appointments.
Public posting is a privacy violation.
Mail is slow and unreliable for short notice; one-day mailing is not practical.

questions.q: Which is the most appropriate way to handle an angry patient who is upset about waiting?
options: Argue about whose fault it is
Listen, apologize for the delay, provide an estimated wait time, and offer options if available
Ignore the patient until they calm down
Disclose another patient's reason for delay
questions.explain: De-escalation includes listening, acknowledging, and offering information and options without revealing other patients' information.
optionExplanations: Arguing escalates conflict and harms service recovery.
Acknowledging, giving an estimate, and offering options is a standard customer-service approach.
Ignoring can increase frustration.
Explaining another patient's situation would breach confidentiality.

questions.q: Which is a key element of an effective exposure control plan under OSHA?
options: A list of patient diagnoses
Procedures for handling sharps and post-exposure follow-up
Provider fee schedules
Insurance benefit summaries
questions.explain: Exposure control plans include engineering/work practice controls, PPE, sharps handling, and steps to take after an exposure incident.
optionExplanations: Patient diagnoses are not part of staff exposure control plans.
Sharps handling and post-exposure procedures are central plan components.
Fee schedules are financial documents.
Benefits summaries relate to insurance, not occupational exposure control.

questions.q: Which role is typically responsible for ensuring CPT and ICD-10 codes are supported by documentation before billing?
options: Environmental services
Coding/billing staff working with clinicians as needed
Patients in the waiting room
The parking attendant
questions.explain: Coding and billing staff validate codes against documentation and payer rules, often querying clinicians for clarification when needed.
optionExplanations: Environmental services focuses on cleaning and facility support.
Coding/billing staff are responsible for code accuracy and documentation support checks.
Patients do not code their own encounters in standard workflows.
Parking attendants are not involved in billing.

questions.q: Which practice best supports accurate time-based billing (when applicable) for certain services?
options: Estimating time from memory days later
Documenting start/stop times or total time on the date of service per policy
Leaving time blank because codes are enough
Recording time only if the patient requests it
questions.explain: Time-based services require contemporaneous documentation of time according to payer and coding rules to support billing.
optionExplanations: Delayed estimates are unreliable and may not meet audit standards.
Documenting time on the date of service supports compliance for time-based codes.
Time cannot be omitted when time determines code selection.
Patient request is not the criterion; payer rules are.

questions.q: Which is a common administrative use of the term 'utilization review'?
options: Deciding which staff member answers phones
Evaluating whether services are medically necessary and appropriate under coverage rules
Choosing which brand of printer to buy
Assigning exam rooms randomly
questions.explain: Utilization review evaluates appropriateness and medical necessity for services, often tied to payer coverage and authorization decisions.
optionExplanations: Phone coverage is staffing, not utilization review.
This matches the standard meaning of utilization review.
Printer choices are procurement decisions.
Random room assignment is unrelated.

questions.q: What is the best definition of a 'referral' in healthcare administration?
options: A patient paying a bill
A provider's recommendation and request for a patient to receive services from another provider or facility
A change of office address
A type of insurance denial
questions.explain: A referral is a formal request from one provider to another for evaluation or treatment, often required for coverage in some plan types.
optionExplanations: Billing payments are not referrals.
This is the correct definition of a clinical/administrative referral.
Address changes are administrative updates, not referrals.
Denials are payer decisions, not referrals.

questions.q: Which action best supports compliance with records retention requirements?
options: Deleting records as soon as the balance is paid
Following a written retention schedule based on applicable laws and contracts, and documenting destruction when permitted
Keeping all records forever with no organization
Storing PHI on personal USB drives
questions.explain: Retention schedules align with legal and contractual requirements; controlled destruction with documentation reduces risk when records are eligible to be disposed.
optionExplanations: Payment status does not determine legal retention requirements.
A written retention schedule and documented destruction are best practice.
Keeping everything forever increases risk, cost, and retrieval problems.
Personal USB storage is insecure and often prohibited.

questions.q: Which is the best example of 'front-end' revenue cycle activity?
options: Appealing a denied claim
Verifying eligibility and collecting copays at check-in
Posting payer payments
Sending accounts to collections
questions.explain: Front-end activities occur before or at the visit and include registration, eligibility verification, and point-of-service collections.
optionExplanations: Appeals occur after denial, which is back-end work.
Eligibility verification and POS collections are classic front-end activities.
Payment posting is mid/back-end after adjudication.
Collections is a late-stage back-end activity.

questions.q: Which is the best description of 'back-end' revenue cycle activity?
options: Scheduling new appointments
Claim follow-up, denial appeals, and patient collections after adjudication
Taking vital signs
Rooming patients
questions.explain: Back-end tasks happen after claims are submitted and processed, focusing on payment posting, follow-up, and collections.
optionExplanations: Scheduling is a front-office operation, not back-end billing.
Follow-up, appeals, and collections are back-end revenue cycle functions.
Vital signs are clinical support tasks.
Rooming patients is clinical flow, not revenue cycle back-end work.

questions.q: Which is the best way to protect PHI when printing documents at the front desk?
options: Use any printer and leave documents in the output tray
Use secure print release (badge/PIN) and retrieve printouts immediately
Email the document to a personal account to print later
Print extra copies in case someone loses one
questions.explain: Secure print release and immediate retrieval reduce the chance that PHI is viewed by unauthorized people.
optionExplanations: Unattended printouts are a common source of privacy incidents.
Secure release and prompt pickup are strong administrative safeguards.
Personal email introduces security and policy issues.
Extra copies increase exposure risk.

questions.q: A patient asks for an estimate of out-of-pocket cost for a procedure. Which information is most important to provide a reasonable estimate?
options: Only the provider's standard charge
The payer's allowed amount and the patient's remaining deductible/coinsurance and copay rules
The office rent cost
The patient's social media profile
questions.explain: Out-of-pocket estimates depend on allowed amounts and the patient's benefits (deductible remaining, coinsurance, copay), not just the provider's sticker price.
optionExplanations: Standard charges may not match allowed amounts and do not reflect benefits.
Allowed amount plus benefits information supports a realistic estimate.
Office overhead does not determine patient responsibility under insurance.
Social media is irrelevant.

questions.q: Which is the best example of a 'quality assurance' activity in a healthcare office?
options: Ignoring patient complaints
Reviewing a sample of charts for documentation completeness and correcting recurring issues
Reducing hand hygiene compliance checks
Sharing passwords to speed work
questions.explain: Quality assurance includes monitoring processes (like documentation) and implementing corrective actions to improve performance and compliance.
optionExplanations: Ignoring complaints undermines improvement.
Chart audits for completeness and corrective action are standard QA activities.
Reducing safety checks increases risk.
Sharing passwords harms security and compliance.

questions.q: Which action best supports patient-centered communication for individuals with limited English proficiency?
options: Use family members as interpreters for all conversations
Offer qualified interpreter services and provide translated key documents when available
Speak louder in English
Avoid discussing treatment options
questions.explain: Qualified interpreter services improve understanding and reduce errors; using family members can introduce privacy and accuracy concerns.
optionExplanations: Family interpreters may be inappropriate and can compromise privacy/accuracy; qualified services are preferred.
Interpreter services and translated materials support informed decisions.
Volume does not improve comprehension of a language barrier.
Avoiding options undermines informed consent and patient rights.

questions.q: Which is a common administrative control for protecting electronic PHI (ePHI) on mobile devices?
options: Disabling passwords to speed access
Using device encryption and remote wipe capabilities under a mobile device policy
Sharing devices among staff without logins
Turning off updates permanently
questions.explain: Encryption and remote wipe reduce risk if a device is lost or stolen and are common safeguards in mobile device policies.
optionExplanations: Removing passwords reduces security.
Encryption and remote wipe are widely used controls for ePHI protection.
Shared devices without logins prevent accountability and increase unauthorized access risk.
Disabling updates can leave security vulnerabilities unpatched.

questions.q: Which is the best description of 'incident-to' billing in a physician office context (general concept)?
options: Billing a service as if performed by a physician under specific supervision and payer rules
Billing only for supplies
Billing a patient for parking
Billing only for inpatient stays
questions.explain: Incident-to billing allows certain services performed by auxiliary personnel to be billed under a physician/NPI when supervision and documentation requirements are met (subject to payer rules).
optionExplanations: This captures the general concept; details depend on payer and setting rules.
Supplies may be billed, but incident-to is not limited to supplies.
Parking is not a medical service billing concept.
Incident-to is generally an outpatient/professional billing concept, not inpatient-only.

questions.q: Which report would be most useful to identify which payers are taking the longest to pay claims?
options: Payer turnaround time report by days from submission to payment
Clinic floor plan
Employee directory
Patient satisfaction survey
questions.explain: A payer turnaround time report shows average days to payment by payer, helping target follow-up and contracting discussions.
optionExplanations: This report directly measures payment speed by payer.
Floor plans do not show payment behavior.
Directories do not track claims.
Satisfaction surveys are not payer payment metrics.

questions.q: Which is the best example of a compliant email practice when communicating with a patient about scheduling?
options: Emailing the full visit note in plain text
Using an approved secure messaging/portal system or encrypted email per policy and limiting details
Sending the patient's diagnosis to a group email list
Including the patient's Social Security number for identification
questions.explain: Secure messaging or encryption with limited details reduces privacy risk and aligns with common HIPAA safeguard practices.
optionExplanations: Sending full clinical notes in plain text email is high risk and often noncompliant with policy.
Using approved secure channels and limiting content is best practice.
Group emails can disclose PHI to others.
SSNs are highly sensitive and should not be shared unnecessarily.

questions.q: In a typical clinic, which document is used to record a patient's consent to treat and general office policies (often separate from procedure-specific consent)?
options: General consent for treatment form
EOB
A/R aging report
OSHA exposure log
questions.explain: Many practices use a general consent form to document consent to treat and acknowledgment of office policies; procedures may require additional informed consent forms.
optionExplanations: General consent forms cover broad consent and acknowledgments.
EOBs are insurer statements, not consent documents.
A/R aging is a billing report.
Exposure logs track employee exposures, not patient consent.

questions.q: Which is the best reason to maintain a backup and downtime procedure for an EHR?
options: To avoid training staff
To continue safe operations and documentation if the system is unavailable
To eliminate the need for privacy safeguards
To increase claim denials
questions.explain: Downtime procedures ensure patient care and critical documentation can continue during outages and that data can be reconciled afterward.
optionExplanations: Training is still required regardless of downtime plans.
Backup and downtime planning supports continuity of operations and patient safety.
Safeguards are still required; downtime can increase risk if unmanaged.
Denials increase with poor documentation; downtime planning aims to prevent that.

questions.q: Which step is most appropriate when correcting an error in a patient's demographic record?
options: Delete the entire patient record
Follow the system's correction process, document the change, and preserve auditability
Ask another patient for the correct information
Ignore it because demographics do not affect billing
questions.explain: Correcting demographics should be done through controlled edits that maintain audit trails and prevent mismatches in claims and clinical records.
optionExplanations: Deleting records can create safety and legal issues.
Controlled correction with auditability is the appropriate approach.
Another patient's information is not a valid source and creates privacy risk.
Demographics directly affect billing, identification, and communication.

questions.q: Which is a common purpose of claim 'scrubbing' software?
options: To remove diagnoses from claims
To check claims for missing/invalid data and coding edits before submission
To replace the need for eligibility verification
To generate clinical treatment plans
questions.explain: Claim scrubbers apply edits and rules to catch errors (missing modifiers, invalid codes, demographic issues) before the claim is sent.
optionExplanations: Diagnoses are required for claims; scrubbers do not remove them.
Pre-submission edits are the main purpose of claim scrubbing.
Eligibility verification is still needed; scrubbers do not confirm coverage.
Treatment plans are clinical, not claim scrubbing outputs.

questions.q: Which is the best definition of 'coordination of benefits' (COB)?
options: Choosing the cheapest appointment time
Determining which insurance plan pays first when a patient has multiple coverages
A method for coding procedures
A requirement to use telehealth
questions.explain: COB rules determine primary vs secondary payer responsibility when more than one plan covers the patient.
optionExplanations: Appointment time selection is unrelated.
This is the standard meaning of coordination of benefits.
COB is not a coding method.
Telehealth use is not determined by COB.

questions.q: Which scenario most clearly requires coordination of benefits processing?
options: A self-pay patient with no insurance
A patient covered by both a personal employer plan and a spouse's plan
A patient who pays cash for a copay
A patient who changes address
questions.explain: When a patient has two active insurance plans, the office must coordinate which payer is primary and how secondary payment works.
optionExplanations: With no insurance, COB does not apply.
Two active plans require COB determination.
Paying a copay does not imply multiple plans.
Address changes do not require COB.

questions.q: Which is the best reason to collect and document a patient's preferred pharmacy in the registration workflow?
options: To reduce HIPAA requirements
To support accurate e-prescribing and reduce delays in medication fulfillment
To increase deductible amounts
To avoid documenting allergies
questions.explain: Preferred pharmacy information helps route prescriptions correctly and reduces follow-up calls and delays.
optionExplanations: Privacy requirements still apply regardless of pharmacy preference.
Accurate pharmacy selection supports e-prescribing workflows and patient convenience.
Deductibles are plan features and are not affected by pharmacy documentation.
Allergy documentation is still required for safe care.

questions.q: Which is the most appropriate action when a payer requests medical records to support a claim review?
options: Send the entire chart for all years without checking scope
Send only the requested relevant records using the approved secure method and document what was sent
Refuse all requests from payers
Post the records to a public file-sharing link
questions.explain: Offices should disclose only what is needed for the request, use secure transmission, and document the disclosure per policy.
optionExplanations: Over-disclosure increases privacy risk and may exceed the request.
Relevant, minimum-necessary records via secure methods with documentation is appropriate.
Payers may be permitted recipients for payment activities; blanket refusal can cause denials.
Public links are insecure and not appropriate for PHI.

questions.q: Which is a common function of a patient portal in an EHR system?
options: Allowing anyone to view any patient's records
Providing patients access to certain results, messages, and appointment functions after identity verification
Automatically approving prior authorizations
Replacing the need for clinical documentation
questions.explain: Patient portals provide secure, authenticated access to limited information and functions for the patient (and authorized proxies).
optionExplanations: Portals require authentication and do not allow open access.
This describes standard portal functionality and security model.
Prior authorizations are payer processes; portals do not automatically approve them.
Clinical documentation is still created by clinicians; portals do not replace it.

questions.q: Which practice best helps reduce the risk of medication list errors during intake?
options: Skip medication review if the visit is short
Perform medication reconciliation by asking the patient to confirm current medications and doses and updating the record
Assume last year's list is still correct
Only record over-the-counter medications
questions.explain: Medication reconciliation improves accuracy by confirming current meds, doses, and changes at each relevant encounter.
optionExplanations: Skipping review increases error risk.
Confirming and updating the list is the standard reconciliation approach.
Old lists may be outdated and unsafe.
OTC meds can matter, but limiting to OTC misses prescriptions and supplements that affect care.

questions.q: Which scheduling practice best supports equitable access when appointment slots are limited?
options: Give all openings to the first caller with no triage
Use a documented triage policy for urgent needs and maintain a waitlist/cancellation list process
Only schedule patients who pay cash
Cancel all follow-ups indefinitely
questions.explain: A triage policy and waitlist process help prioritize urgent care needs fairly while filling openings efficiently when cancellations occur.
optionExplanations: No triage can delay urgent cases and may be unfair.
Triage and waitlist processes are common, documented methods to manage scarce appointment capacity.
Scheduling based on payment method raises ethical and contractual concerns.
Eliminating follow-ups can harm continuity of care.
